_meta.description: Universe matrix — 13 verticals × 10 universal hardware supply-chain stages. Each cell has entries (label + style class) and optional highlighted analytical detail. Ex-materials.

net: NVLink vs UALink; IB vs Ethernet

eq_wfe.t: Wafer-Fab Equipment — Lithography (the deepest chokepoint in tech)
eq_wfe.b: ASML (Netherlands) is the sole maker of EUV lithography. Within it, Carl Zeiss SMT (Germany) is the exclusive EUV optics supplier and Cymer (ASML-owned, US) the light source — single-source within a monopoly. High-NA EUV (~$380M/tool) is deploying first at Intel (14A, Dec 2025) and Samsung; TSMC is notably holding back, sticking with 0.33-NA EUV + multipatterning for A14/A16. US+NL+Japan jointly restrict advanced tools to China; SMEE is far behind, with sub-10nm scanners unlikely before ~2030.

ff_fdy.t: Foundry — Leading-edge logic (the defining geopolitical risk of the AI era)
ff_fdy.b: TSMC (2330.TW) has ~90%+ of <5nm logic; N2 (2nm nanosheet) entered volume production Q4 2025, A16 (backside power) H2 2026. TSMC Fab 18 in Tainan is the single most strategically important industrial site on earth. Arizona fabs (N4/N3) are ramping for geographic diversification but the centre of gravity remains Taiwan. Samsung (Korea) and Intel (US) are the only other leading-edge players; SMIC (China) is capped at ~7nm by export controls.

bp_pkg.t: Advanced Packaging — TSMC CoWoS (the gating bottleneck on AI GPU output)
bp_pkg.b: CoWoS (chip-on-wafer-on-substrate) is the single capacity constraint that has gated Nvidia/AMD AI GPU shipments since 2023. TSMC is doubling CoWoS capacity yearly (AP6/AP7 in Chiayi) and outsourcing overflow to ASE, Amkor and SPIL. Rubin Ultra was reportedly re-architected over CoWoS-L yield. Secondary chokepoints: ABF substrates (Ibiden, Shinko, Unimicron; Ajinomoto's ABF film is a hidden near-monopoly) and hybrid-bonding tools (BESI). Panel-level packaging (FOPLP) is the next-gen escape.

bp_mem.t: Memory — HBM (THE supply gate on AI accelerators; a Korea chokepoint)
bp_mem.b: High-Bandwidth Memory gates AI GPU shipments and is concentrated in Korea. SK Hynix leads (~62% HBM, Q2 2025) and is Nvidia's lead supplier; Micron ~21% (overtook Samsung); Samsung ~17%, clearing Nvidia HBM4 qualification. Each Blackwell GPU carries ~8 HBM stacks; all three suppliers are sold out for 2026. HBM4 (2026) integrates a logic base die fabbed at TSMC — pulling the foundry into the memory stack. China (CXMT) is sampling HBM3 but years behind.

ds_acc.t: AI Accelerators — Nvidia/CUDA vs the custom-ASIC counter-trend
ds_acc.b: Nvidia holds ~80-90% of merchant AI silicon (Blackwell→Rubin cadence) defended by the CUDA software moat. The structural counter-trend is hyperscaler custom ASICs — Google TPU + Meta MTIA + OpenAI (Broadcom-designed); AWS Trainium + Microsoft Maia (Marvell); Google TPU v7 'Trillium' also co-designed with MediaTek. Broadcom + Marvell control ~95% of custom co-design; custom shipments are projected +44.6% in 2026 (~28% of AI servers). Late-2025: Gemini 3's success and Google's pivot to selling TPUs (Anthropic, Meta-for-training, neoclouds via Fluidstack) marked the counter-trend going mainstream — but Google and Nvidia now compete for the same CoWoS, so the real bottlenecks still sit upstream at TSMC CoWoS and SK Hynix HBM, not in the design houses.

in_opt.t: Optical — EML lasers (the deepest optical chokepoint)
in_opt.b: Fewer than five firms make EML lasers at scale: Lumentum, Coherent, Mitsubishi Electric (6503.T), Sumitomo, Broadcom. Nvidia committed ~$4B (~$2B each to Lumentum + Coherent) in 2026 to lock laser supply, pushing EML lead times past 2027 for everyone else. China dominates transceiver module assembly (~63% global; InnoLight + Eoptolink ~60% of Nvidia 800G) but US silicon (Marvell/Broadcom DSPs) + lasers capture the value. CPO on TSMC COUPE is the 2026 inflection.

in_act.t: Robotics — Precision motion parts (Japan chokepoint, China localizing)
in_act.b: Humanoids need ~28-40 actuators each; the costliest precision parts are historically Japanese chokepoints: harmonic/strain-wave reducers (Harmonic Drive 6324.T), cycloidal/RV reducers (Nabtesco ~60% share), and planetary roller screws (NSK, THK, plus Germany's GSA — the primary Optimus supplier). China is aggressively localizing all three to cut cost: Green Harmonic (688017.SH, targeting Optimus supply), Inovance (motors/drives), and Sanhua (exclusive Tier-1 Optimus actuator assembler). Optimus Gen-3 is reportedly ~70% China-sourced.

bp_dsp.b: Near-eye displays (tiny, ultra-bright, high-PPI) are an unsolved AR bottleneck. No clear winner among microLED (brightest, lowest yield — JBD dominant, plus Porotech, AUO), OLEDoS (premium MR — Sony powers Apple Vision Pro; BOE), and LCoS (mature/cheap for waveguides — Himax, Omnivision inside the Meta Ray-Ban Display). Mass-transfer/efficiency yield on full-color microLED is the long-term endgame problem. Goertek (China) dominates light-engine assembly.

bp_arx.t: AR Optics — Waveguide combiners (the other make-or-break component)
bp_arx.b: Getting the image to the eye in a glasses form factor is the second unsolved AR problem. Reflective/geometric waveguides: Lumus (Israel — inside the $799 Meta Ray-Ban Display). Diffractive: Dispelix, DigiLens, WaveOptics (Snap), Vuzix. Low light-efficiency, rainbow artifacts and yield make this a deep chokepoint. Upstream: high-index glass (Schott supplies Ray-Ban Display glass; Corning, Ohara, Hoya) and emerging SiC waveguides (index ~2.7, used in Meta Orion; no mature commercial supply chain yet).

ip_eda.t: EDA & IP — US duopoly + Arm (a fast on/off geopolitical lever)
ip_eda.b: Synopsys + Cadence (~70%+ of design software) plus Arm's CPU IP gate virtually all advanced design — the highest-margin, most defensible layer of the universe. Proven to be a fast on/off lever: BIS halted EDA exports to China in May 2025 and reversed it ~July 2025 in a rare-earth-for-tools trade. China's Empyrean (301269.SZ) cannot match full-flow leading-edge. RISC-V is rising as a license-risk hedge, China-favored.

in_fdy.t: Foundry inputs — 300mm silicon wafers (a quiet Japan chokepoint)
in_fdy.b: Shin-Etsu (4063.T) + SUMCO (3436.T) of Japan supply ~50%+ of the world's 300mm polished/epi wafers; GlobalWafers (Taiwan) and Siltronic (Germany) round out the oligopoly. Every leading-edge chip starts on one of these wafers. Upstream of even TSMC, this is one of the least-discussed but hardest-to-replace Japanese strongholds — mirroring Japan's grip on photoresist and precision reducers.

si_dci.t: Datacenter Infrastructure — Taiwan ODMs + the power/cooling wall
si_dci.b: Taiwan ODMs build the world's AI servers: Foxconn, Quanta, Wiwynn (~50% of hyperscale AI-server procurement), Wistron, Inventec. The binding constraint has shifted from chips to power and cooling: liquid cooling jumps from ~15% (2024) to ~76% of the mix by 2026 (GB200 mandates it), CDU capacity is the cooling bottleneck, and grid access + multi-year transformer lead times gate the buildout. This is the deepest cross-link into the energy supply chain.

integrated_companies: TSMC (foundry + CoWoS packaging + COUPE silicon photonics) · Samsung (DRAM/HBM/NAND + foundry + OLEDoS display) · Broadcom AVGO (custom ASIC + switch silicon + optical DSP + CPO) · Nvidia NVDA (GPU + NVLink + BlueField DPU + Jetson Thor robotics + ~$4B laser investment) · Foxconn 2317.TW (server ODM + components + EV) · Sony 6758.T (CMOS image sensors + OLEDoS micro-displays + lasers) · Goertek 002241.SZ (acoustics + AR/VR light engines + device assembly) · Inovance 300124.SZ (motors + drives + screws + humanoid actuators)
